participants.reasoning: The extracted text matches the official participant criteria and overall baseline characteristics, with only minor additional details not in conflict.
participants.extracted: Adults with relapsing forms of multiple sclerosis were enrolled from 127 centers in 21 countries. Eligible patients were 18 to 55 years old, met McDonald criteria for MS, had an ambulatory status with EDSS score ≤5.5, and had at least 1 relapse in the prior year or at least 2 relapses in the previous 2 years, with no relapse in the 60 days before randomization. The randomized population included 1088 patients: 363 assigned to placebo, 366 to teriflunomide 7 mg, and 359 to teriflunomide 14 mg; 1086 patients received study treatment and formed the modified intention-to-treat population. The study population was predominantly female (72.2%), Caucasian (97.5%), and had a mean age of 38 years; most had EDSS scores ≤3.5, two or fewer relapses in the prior 2 years, and relapsing-remitting MS, although 8.6% had secondary progressive or progressive relapsing MS.
participants.official: Age: 18 to 55 years; mixed sample: 91% RRMS and 9% PMS; mean disease duration 9 years; mean EDSS 2.7; prior use of DMT in the previous 2 years: 27.0% (28.4% in teriflunomide 14 mg, 27.9% in teriflunomide 7 mg, and 24.8% in placebo)

interventions.reasoning: The extracted text matches the official interventions in identifying teriflunomide 7 mg and 14 mg once daily and placebo over a similar treatment duration.
interventions.extracted: Teriflunomide, an oral disease-modifying therapy for relapsing multiple sclerosis, was evaluated in two once-daily doses: 7 mg and 14 mg, compared with placebo over a 108-week treatment period.
interventions.official:
Teriflunomide 14 mg oral capsule once daily for 25 months (n = 359)
Teriflunomide 7 mg oral capsule once daily for 25 months (n = 366)
Placebo oral capsule once daily for 25 months (n = 363)

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes emphasize ARR over 108 weeks and 12-week confirmed disability progression, whereas the official outcomes specify relapse at 12 and 24 months, disability worsening at 24 months, and safety/withdrawal outcomes, so the timeframes and endpoints do not match.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure annualized relapse rate (ARR), defined as the number of confirmed relapses per patient-year, over the 108-week treatment period. It also planned to measure sustained disability progression, assessed as 12-week confirmed disability progression on the Expanded Disability Status Scale (EDSS), defined as an increase from baseline of at least 1.0 point for patients with baseline EDSS ≤5.5 or at least 0.5 points for patients with baseline EDSS >5.5, with disability status evaluated through week 108. Additional planned assessments included MRI endpoints and safety/tolerability, but the primary and key secondary clinical outcomes were ARR and 12-week confirmed disability progression.
outcomes.official: Relapse 12 and 24 months, disability worsening at 24 months, withdrawals due to AEs, SAEs
